claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the child's age, and we'll continue from there.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have two children I'm asking about - Alex who is 17, and Blake who is 18.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children:
- **Alex**, aged 17
- **Blake**, aged 18

Since both children are between 16 and 19 years old, I'll need to check if they meet the education or training requirements. Let me navigate to the appropriate check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For children aged 16-19, eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

**Let me ask about each child:**

**For Alex (17) and Blake (18):**

For each of them, please tell me which of the following applies:

1. **Currently in education or training** (e.g., attending school, college, or an approved training course)
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last few months)
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Are Alex and Blake currently in education or training, or have they recently left?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex is still studying - he's doing his A-levels at sixth form college. 

Blake finished school though - he left after his GCSEs and isn't studying anymore.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details. Let me assess each child separately.

**For Alex (17)** - currently doing A-levels at sixth form college, so he's in education. I need to check if this qualifies.

**For Blake (18)** - left after GCSEs and isn't studying anymore. I need to check if there might be any extension period eligibility.

Let me navigate through the appropriate checks for both children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is helpful. **A-levels are on the approved qualifications list**, so Alex's education qualifies as non-advanced education.

**A couple of quick questions about Alex:**

1. Is his A-level course **full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. Is the course **funded by an employer** or linked to a guaranteed job with an employer afterwards?

---

Now let me also check Blake's situation - when did Blake leave school after his GCSEs? This will help determine if there's any extension period that might apply.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. **For Blake (18)**: The extension period (up to 20 weeks after leaving education) **only applies until the child turns 18**. Since Blake is already 18, this extension period wouldn't help unfortunately.

---

**So, to summarise what I need to confirm:**

**For Alex (17, doing A-levels):**
1. Is his A-level course **full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. Is the course funded by an employer, or linked to a guaranteed job with an employer?

**For Blake (18):**
Based on what you've told me, Blake has left education after GCSEs, isn't currently studying, and is 18 years old. Unfortunately, the extension period for those who've left education only applies until the child turns 18 - so this wouldn't apply to Blake.

Could you please answer the questions about Alex, and I'll also confirm: is there anything else about Blake's situation I should know - for example, is he perhaps considering starting any approved education or training soon?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - yes, it's definitely full-time, he's at college most days of the week. And no, it's not employer-funded or anything like that, it's just regular sixth form.

As for Blake, no, he's not planning to go back into education or training at the moment. He's just been working actually - he got a full-time job at the local supermarket.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details. That's very helpful.

**For Alex (17):** Great news - A-levels at sixth form college, full-time, and not employer-funded all meet the approved education criteria. Let me continue checking the remaining requirements for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, I need to check Alex's work situation now.

**For Alex (17):** Does Alex work at all alongside his A-levels? If so, does he work **less than 24 hours per week**?

(It's fine if he has a part-time job - the key is whether it's under 24 hours per week, or if he doesn't work at all.)

---

**For Blake (18):** You mentioned he has a full-time job at the supermarket. Unfortunately, this confirms that Blake doesn't meet the eligibility criteria. Children aged 16-19 need to be in approved education or training to qualify, and Blake has left education, isn't planning to return, and is working full-time.

Once you answer about Alex, I'll be able to give you a complete picture of your eligibility for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Alex doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies.

So does that mean I can only claim for Alex then, not for Blake?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress. Just a couple more checks for Alex:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** Specifically, is he claiming Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit for himself?

(Most students wouldn't be, but I need to confirm this.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Alex is doing A-levels, so he's not in an apprenticeship - that's fine. Now I need to check a few things about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

(If not, there are other ways you might still qualify - for example, through EU Settlement Scheme status, or if you're a Crown servant living abroad.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Manchester counts as normally living in the UK. ✓

Now, **are you responsible for Alex?** This can be met in any of these ways:
- **You live with Alex**, OR
- **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, is there another parent or guardian who might be claiming for him? 

(If both parents want to claim, only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! Just one more thing to check:

**Is Alex currently living at home normally with you?** Or is he in any of these special situations:
- In hospital for an extended stay
- In local authority care
- In a fostering arrangement

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's just living at home normally with me, nothing special like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(In most cases where a child lives at home normally, the answer would be no - but I need to confirm this.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I've now completed the assessment for both children. Let me provide you with the final outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Results

### ✅ Alex (17) — ELIGIBLE

You **can** claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the requirements because:
- He's in approved education (A-levels are a qualifying course)
- His studies are full-time and not employer-funded
- He doesn't work or receive benefits in his own right
- You live in the UK and he lives at home with you
- No one else is claiming for him

### ❌ Blake (18) — NOT ELIGIBLE

Unfortunately, you **cannot** claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- At 16-19, children need to be in approved education or training to qualify
- Blake left school after his GCSEs and isn't currently studying
- The 20-week extension period (for those who recently left education) only applies until the child turns 18, and Blake is already 18
- He's also working full-time, which would disqualify him even if he were in education

---

## Next Steps for Claiming for Alex

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide documents**: Your proof of identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. **Be aware**: If you or a partner earn over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply (though you can still claim and receive National Insurance credits)
4. **Payments**: Usually made every 4 weeks

---

I'm sorry you can't claim for Blake, but if his circumstances change (for example, if he decides to return to approved education or training before he turns 20), you could potentially claim for him again at that point. Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify?

reasonings: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved, non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college); (2) His course is full-time (at least 12 hours per week); (3) The course is not employer-funded; (4) He does not work (under the 24 hours/week limit); (5) He does not receive benefits in his own right; (6) He is not in an apprenticeship; (7) You live in the UK; (8) You are responsible for him (he lives with you); (9) No one else is claiming for him; (10) He lives at home normally; and (11) The council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19, which means he would need to be in approved education or training to qualify; (2) He left education after GCSEs and is not currently studying or training; (3) He is working full-time at a supermarket; (4) The 20-week extension period for those who have recently left education only applies until the child turns 18, and Blake is already 18. Since Blake is not in approved education or training and the extension period does not apply, he does not meet the eligibility criteria.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, who meets all eligibility requirements as a 17-year-old in full-time approved education. Unfortunately, you cannot claim for Blake, as he is 18, not in education or training, and working full-time.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved, non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college); (2) His course is full-time (at least 12 hours per week); (3) The course is not employer-funded; (4) He does not work (under the 24 hours/week limit); (5) He does not receive benefits in his own right; (6) He is not in an apprenticeship; (7) You live in the UK; (8) You are responsible for him (he lives with you); (9) No one else is claiming for him; (10) He lives at home normally; and (11) The council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19, which means he would need to be in approved education or training to qualify; (2) He left education after GCSEs and is not currently studying or training; (3) He is working full-time at a supermarket; (4) The 20-week extension period for those who have recently left education only applies until the child turns 18, and Blake is already 18. Since Blake is not in approved education or training and the extension period does not apply, he does not meet the eligibility criteria.

response.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, who meets all eligibility requirements as a 17-year-old in full-time approved education. Unfortunately, you cannot claim for Blake, as he is 18, not in education or training, and working full-time.
